attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections and progressive fatigue. She has a medical history of rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed five years ago. Her medications include methotrexate and folic acid. On examination, she appears pale with vital signs within normal limits. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Investigations reveal:

  • haemoglobin 105 g/L (115-165)
  • white cell count 2.8 × 10⁹/L (4.0-11.0)
  • neutrophils 0.8 × 10⁹/L (2.0-7.5)
  • lymphocytes 1.6 × 10⁹/L (1.0-4.0)
  • platelets 145 × 10⁹/L (150-400)

A peripheral blood film is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on the peripheral blood film?
attributes.explanation:
The peripheral blood film shows large lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm containing azurophilic granules, which is characteristic of T-cell large granular lymphocytic (T-LGL) leukaemia. This finding, combined with the clinical presentation of neutropenia and recurrent infections in a patient with rheumatoid arthritis, strongly supports this diagnosis.

T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukaemia is a rare chronic lymphoproliferative disorder characterised by a clonal expansion of cytotoxic T-cells. The peripheral blood typically shows large lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm containing azurophilic granules. Neutropenia is the most common cytopenia, occurring in 70-80% of patients, and is the main cause of recurrent infections. There is a strong association with autoimmune disorders, particularly rheumatoid arthritis, which is present in 25-30% of cases. The clinical course is usually indolent, with the main complications related to neutropenia.

Felty syndrome is a rare complication of rheumatoid arthritis characterised by the triad of rheumatoid arthritis, neutropenia, and splenomegaly. While this patient has rheumatoid arthritis and neutropenia, the absence of splenomegaly and the presence of characteristic large granular lymphocytes on the blood film make T-LGL leukaemia more likely. Additionally, T-LGL leukaemia is often misdiagnosed as Felty syndrome due to the overlapping features.

Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL) typically shows small, mature-appearing lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm and clumped chromatin. Smudge cells are often present. The lymphocytes in CLL do not contain azurophilic granules, which distinguishes them from the large granular lymphocytes seen in this case. Additionally, CLL typically presents with lymphocytosis rather than neutropenia as the predominant finding.

Reactive lymphocytosis can show atypical lymphocytes with increased cytoplasm, but these cells typically lack the consistent morphology and azurophilic granules seen in T-LGL leukaemia. Reactive lymphocytosis is usually transient and associated with viral infections or other inflammatory conditions. The persistent neutropenia and association with rheumatoid arthritis in this case are more consistent with T-LGL leukaemia.

Hairy cell leukaemia is characterised by lymphocytes with hair-like cytoplasmic projections and a distinctive cytoplasmic appearance. These cells typically have an oval or indented nucleus and abundant pale blue cytoplasm without the distinct azurophilic granules seen in T-LGL leukaemia. Pancytopenia and splenomegaly are common findings, which are not present in this case.
learningPoints: T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukaemia is characterised by clonal expansion of cytotoxic T-cells with azurophilic granules in the cytoplasm.
Peripheral blood smear in T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukaemia typically shows large lymphocytes with abundant cytoplasm and azurophilic granules.
Neutropenia is the most common cytopenia in T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukaemia, occurring in 70-80% of patients.
references: Uthman, Ed. T-Cell Large Granular Lymphocytic Leukemia. Flikr. Accessed under license CC By 2.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/deed.en. Accessed at: https://www.flickr.com/photos/euthman/49244546086/in/pool-labmed/
Shi M, Jevremovic D. T cell large granular lymphocytic leukemia. PathologyOutlines.com. https://www.pathologyoutlines.com/topic/lymphomanonBtLGL.html. Accessed 28 Jun 2025.
Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020; Chaptet 13, pages 605-607.
